title: Lamb Ouzi Whole Slow Roasted

seo.title: Lamb Ouzi Recipe: Whole Slow Roasted Middle Eastern Feast
seo.metaDescription: Master the art of Middle Eastern Lamb Ouzi with our whole slow roasted recipe. Perfect for a feast, this authentic dish is easier than you think!
longTailKeywords: how to make whole slow roasted lamb ouzi
authentic middle eastern lamb ouzi recipe
easy lamb ouzi whole slow roasted
lowCompetitionKeywords: traditional whole slow roasted lamb ouzi
seo.h1: Authentic Lamb Ouzi: Whole Slow Roasted Middle Eastern Delight
h2Headings: Ingredients for Whole Slow Roasted Lamb Ouzi
Step-by-Step Guide to Making Lamb Ouzi
Tips for Perfect Slow Roasted Lamb
seo.imageAlt: Whole slow roasted Lamb Ouzi, a traditional Middle Eastern dish, garnished with nuts and fresh herbs.
seo.imageCaption: Our magnificent whole slow roasted Lamb Ouzi, a centerpiece for any Middle Eastern feast.

description: Lamb Ouzi is a majestic Middle Eastern dish, featuring tender, slow-roasted lamb infused with aromatic spices, served traditionally over a bed of spiced rice. This grand recipe guides you to create a succulent whole lamb, perfect for celebrations and large family gatherings. The meat becomes incredibly fall-off-the-bone tender, absorbing all the rich flavors of the marinade, making every bite a culinary delight.

ingredients.notes: bone-in, skin on preferred for flavor

instructions.title: Prepare the Lamb for Marination
instructions.text: Pat the whole lamb leg or shoulder completely dry with paper towels. Using a sharp knife, make several deep incisions all over the lamb to allow the marinade to penetrate. In a bowl, combine 2 tablespoons of seven spice, 1 teaspoon of ground cinnamon, 1/2 teaspoon of ground cardamom, 1/4 cup of ghee or olive oil, minced garlic, and a generous amount of salt and black pepper. Rub this mixture thoroughly into the lamb, ensuring it gets into all the incisions. Place the lamb in a large roasting pan, cover, and refrigerate for at least 4 hours, preferably overnight, to allow the flavors to meld.

instructions.title: Slow Roast the Lamb
instructions.text: Preheat your oven to 150°C (300°F). Add 2 cups of water or broth to the bottom of the roasting pan around the lamb. Cover the pan tightly with foil, ensuring no steam escapes. Place in the preheated oven and slow roast for 3-4 hours. After this time, remove the foil, baste the lamb with its juices, and continue roasting uncovered for another 1-2 hours, or until the lamb is fork-tender and deeply golden brown. The internal temperature should reach 63°C (145°F) for medium-rare, or higher for well-done.

instructions.title: Prepare the Spiced Rice Base
instructions.text: While the lamb is roasting during its final hour, begin preparing the rice. In a large pot, heat 1/4 cup of ghee or olive oil over medium heat. Add the chopped onions and sauté until softened and translucent, about 8-10 minutes. Add the remaining 1 tablespoon of seven spice, 1/2 teaspoon of ground cinnamon, 1/2 teaspoon of ground cardamom, and bay leaves. Stir and cook for 1 minute until fragrant.

instructions.title: Cook the Basmati Rice
instructions.text: Drain the soaked basmati rice and add it to the pot with the sautéed onions and spices. Stir gently to coat the rice grains evenly. Pour in the warm chicken or lamb broth and season with salt to taste. Bring the mixture to a boil, then reduce the heat to low, cover the pot tightly, and simmer for 18-20 minutes, or until all the liquid is absorbed and the rice is tender. Do not lift the lid during cooking.

instructions.title: Rest the Lamb and Fluff the Rice
instructions.text: Once the lamb is cooked, carefully remove it from the oven and transfer it to a large cutting board. Tent it loosely with foil and let it rest for at least 20-30 minutes. This allows the juices to redistribute, resulting in a more tender and flavorful meat. While the lamb rests, remove the bay leaves from the rice and gently fluff the rice with a fork.

instructions.title: Assemble and Garnish for Serving
instructions.text: To serve, spread the spiced basmati rice generously onto a large serving platter. Carefully place the whole slow-roasted lamb leg or shoulder on top of the bed of rice. Garnish lavishly with toasted pine nuts, fried or toasted blanched almonds, and freshly chopped parsley. Serve immediately with a side of yogurt or a fresh salad for a truly authentic Middle Eastern feast.

tips: For an even deeper flavor, marinate the lamb for up to 24 hours in the refrigerator.
If you don't have Seven Spice, you can create a similar blend using equal parts ground allspice, black pepper, cinnamon, coriander, cumin, cloves, and nutmeg.
Always allow the lamb to rest after roasting; this is crucial for tender, juicy meat. Don't skip this step!
article:
Embark on a culinary journey to the heart of the Middle East with a dish that embodies hospitality, festivity, and incredible flavor: Whole Slow Roasted Lamb Ouzi. This magnificent centerpiece, often reserved for special occasions and grand gatherings, transforms a simple cut of lamb into an unforgettable feast. Imagine tender, fall-off-the-bone lamb, fragrant with aromatic spices, cradling a rich, savory rice stuffing studded with nuts and often ground meat. Preparing Lamb Ouzi is a labor of love, but the result is a show-stopping meal that will impress even the most discerning palates.

## What is Lamb Ouzi?

Lamb Ouzi, also known as Qouzi or Ghozi in various Middle Eastern dialects, is a traditional and highly revered dish celebrated across the Levant and Mesopotamia. At its core, Ouzi refers to a whole roasted lamb or a large cut of lamb (like a leg or shoulder) that is typically stuffed with a spiced rice mixture. This stuffing often includes ground meat (lamb or beef), toasted nuts such as pine nuts and almonds, and a medley of warm spices like allspice, cardamom, and cinnamon. The dish is a hallmark of generosity and celebration, frequently served at weddings, Eid festivities, and large family gatherings. The magic of Lamb Ouzi lies in its slow roasting process, which allows the meat to become incredibly tender and infused with deep flavors, while the rice absorbs the rich juices, making it exceptionally moist and aromatic. It’s more than just a meal; it's an experience, a symbol of Middle Eastern culinary tradition and warm hospitality.

## Ingredients for Whole Slow Roasted Lamb Ouzi

To create an authentic and memorable Whole Slow Roasted Lamb Ouzi, selecting quality ingredients is paramount. Here's what you'll need:

For the Lamb:
*   **Whole Leg or Shoulder of Lamb:** Approximately 5-8 pounds (2.5-4 kg) bone-in. For a truly grand Ouzi, a small whole lamb can be used if you have the equipment.
*   **Marinade/Rub:**
    *   1/4 cup olive oil
    *   4-6 cloves garlic, minced
    *   1 large onion, grated or finely chopped
    *   1/4 cup lemon juice
    *   1/2 cup plain yogurt (optional, but great for tenderizing)
    *   2 tablespoons mixed spice blend (such as Baharat, or a mix of allspice, cinnamon, cardamom, nutmeg, black pepper)
    *   1 tablespoon salt
    *   1 teaspoon black pepper

For the Rice Stuffing:
*   2 cups Basmati rice, rinsed
*   1 pound ground lamb or beef (optional, but highly recommended)
*   1 large onion, finely chopped
*   1/4 cup olive oil
*   1/2 cup toasted pine nuts
*   1/2 cup toasted slivered almonds
*   1/4 cup dried cranberries or raisins (optional, for a touch of sweetness)
*   2 tablespoons mixed spice blend (same as above, or to taste)
*   1 teaspoon ground cinnamon
*   1 teaspoon salt
*   1/2 teaspoon black pepper
*   3-4 cups lamb or chicken broth

For Roasting & Garnish:
*   2-3 cups lamb or chicken broth (for the roasting pan)
*   Fresh parsley, chopped (for garnish)
*   Extra toasted nuts (for garnish)

## Step-by-Step Guide to Making Lamb Ouzi

Crafting the perfect Lamb Ouzi is a process that rewards patience and attention to detail.

1.  **Prepare the Lamb:**
    *   Rinse the lamb thoroughly and pat it dry with paper towels. This helps the marinade adhere better.
    *   Using a sharp knife, score the surface of the lamb in a diamond pattern, making shallow cuts about 1/2 inch deep. This allows the marinade to penetrate deeply.
    *   In a bowl, combine olive oil, minced garlic, grated onion, lemon juice, yogurt (if using), spice blend, salt, and pepper. Mix well to form a thick paste.
    *   Generously rub the marinade all over the lamb, ensuring it gets into all the scored cuts. For best results, place the lamb in a large roasting bag or cover it tightly and refrigerate for at least 6 hours, or ideally overnight (up to 24 hours).

2.  **Prepare the Rice Stuffing:**
    *   Heat 1/4 cup olive oil in a large pot or deep skillet over medium heat.
    *   Add the chopped onion and sauté until softened and translucent, about 5-7 minutes.
    *   If using ground meat, add it to the pot and cook, breaking it up with a spoon, until browned. Drain any excess fat.
    *   Stir in the mixed spice blend, cinnamon, salt, and pepper. Cook for another minute until fragrant.
    *   Add the rinsed Basmati rice to the pot and stir to coat it with the spices and meat. Sauté for 2-3 minutes.
    *   Pour in 3 cups of lamb or chicken broth. Bring to a boil, then reduce heat to low, cover, and simmer for about 10-12 minutes, or until the rice is partially cooked (al dente). It will finish cooking inside the lamb.
    *   Remove from heat and gently stir in the toasted pine nuts, almonds, and dried cranberries/raisins (if using). Set aside to cool slightly.

3.  **Stuff and Roast the Lamb:**
    *   Preheat your oven to 300°F (150°C).
    *   Carefully stuff the cavity of the lamb with the prepared rice mixture. Don't overpack it, as the rice will expand further.
    *   If the lamb has a large opening, you can sew it shut with kitchen twine and a large needle, or use metal skewers to close the opening.
    *   Place the stuffed lamb in a large roasting pan. Pour 2-3 cups of lamb or chicken broth into the bottom of the pan. This creates a moist environment and prevents the lamb from drying out.
    *   Cover the roasting pan tightly with aluminum foil or a lid.
    *   Roast for 5-8 hours, depending on the size of your lamb. A 5-8 pound leg will likely take 5-6 hours. For a larger piece, it could be longer. The goal is fall-off-the-bone tenderness.
    *   About an hour before the estimated cooking time is up, remove the foil to allow the lamb skin to brown and crisp slightly. Baste with the pan juices.
    *   The lamb is done when it’s incredibly tender and an internal temperature probe reads around 190-200°F (88-93°C) in the thickest part (not touching the bone).

4.  **Rest and Serve:**
    *   Once cooked, remove the lamb from the oven and transfer it to a large cutting board or platter. Tent it loosely with foil and let it rest for at least 30 minutes. This allows the juices to redistribute, ensuring a moist and tender result.
    *   Carefully carve the lamb, ensuring some of the delicious rice stuffing is scooped out with each serving.
    *   Garnish with fresh chopped parsley and extra toasted nuts.

## Tips for Perfect Slow Roasted Lamb

Achieving perfection with your Lamb Ouzi requires a few key techniques:

*   **Marinate, Marinate, Marinate:** The longer the lamb marinates, the more flavor it will absorb, and the more tender it will become. Aim for at least 12 hours, but 24 hours is ideal.
*   **Low and Slow is the Way to Go:** Resist the urge to crank up the heat. A low oven temperature over a long period breaks down the tough fibers in the lamb, resulting in incredibly succulent meat that practically melts in your mouth.
*   **Maintain Moisture:** Keeping the roasting pan covered tightly with foil or a lid traps steam, creating a moist environment that prevents the lamb from drying out. Adding broth to the bottom of the pan also contributes to this. Basting occasionally helps too.
*   **Don't Overstuff the Lamb:** While you want a generous amount of rice, ensure there's enough room for the rice to expand and cook evenly. Overpacking can lead to raw rice in the center.
*   **Resting is Crucial:** After roasting, always allow the lamb to rest. This step is non-negotiable for juicy meat. The juices, which are pushed to the center during cooking, redistribute throughout the meat, resulting in a more tender and flavorful outcome.
*   **Use a Reliable Roasting Pan:** A sturdy, heavy-bottomed roasting pan will ensure even heat distribution.

## Serving Suggestions for Lamb Ouzi

Lamb Ouzi is a feast in itself, traditionally served as the grand centerpiece on a large platter. To enhance the presentation and provide a balanced meal, consider these serving suggestions:

*   **Grand Platter:** Present the whole roasted lamb on a large, ornate platter. Carefully scoop out some of the fragrant rice stuffing and arrange it around the lamb. Garnish generously with fresh chopped parsley, extra toasted pine nuts, and slivered almonds for visual appeal and added texture.
*   **Cooling Dips:** Offer bowls of plain yogurt or creamy labneh (strained yogurt) on the side. Their cool, tangy flavor provides a refreshing contrast to the rich lamb and spiced rice.
*   **Fresh Salads:** Light, vibrant salads cut through the richness of the Ouzi. Fattoush, a bread salad with mixed greens, tomatoes, cucumbers, and a sumac-lemon dressing, or Tabbouleh, a finely chopped parsley and bulgur salad, are excellent choices.
*   **Roasted Vegetables:** Alongside the lamb, you can roast root vegetables like carrots, potatoes, or sweet potatoes in the same pan during the last hour, or serve separate dishes of grilled or steamed vegetables.
*   **Warm Flatbreads:** Freshly baked pita bread or other Middle Eastern flatbreads are perfect for scooping up any remaining juices and rice.

## Variations & Substitutions for Ouzi

While the traditional Lamb Ouzi is a masterpiece, there are many ways to adapt it to your preferences or available ingredients:

*   **Meat Alternatives:** While lamb is classic, similar slow-roasting techniques can be applied to a large cut of goat or even a substantial beef roast for a different flavor profile.
*   **Stuffing Creativity:**
    *   **Rice Types:** While Basmati is common, you can experiment with short-grain rice or even a mix of rice and freekeh (cracked wheat) for a nuttier texture.
    *   **Nuts & Dried Fruits:** Expand beyond pine nuts and almonds. Pistachios, walnuts, or even cashews can be used. For dried fruits, try dried apricots or dates for a deeper sweetness.
    *   **Vegetables:** Add finely diced carrots, peas, or bell peppers to the rice stuffing for extra color and nutrients.
*   **Spice Adjustments:** Feel free to adjust the spice blend to your liking. A pinch of cumin, coriander, or even a touch of smoked paprika can add new dimensions. Some regional variations might include dried lime powder or sumac in the stuffing.
*   **Cooking Methods:**
    *   **Oven Bags:** Using a large oven-roasting bag can help lock in moisture even more effectively, making for an incredibly tender lamb.
    *   **Pressure Cooker:** For smaller cuts of lamb or to significantly reduce cooking time, a large pressure cooker can be used, though it won't achieve the same browning as oven roasting.
*   **Deconstructed Ouzi:** If roasting a whole lamb is too daunting or you’re serving a smaller crowd, you can make a "deconstructed" Ouzi. This involves slow-cooking or braising lamb chunks until fall-apart tender, then serving them over a bed of the spiced rice and garnished with nuts. It captures the essence of the dish without the whole roast.

Making Whole Slow Roasted Lamb Ouzi is an incredibly rewarding culinary experience. It's a dish that speaks volumes of love and celebration, transforming a simple ingredient into a centerpiece that will be remembered long after the last bite. So, gather your ingredients, set aside a day, and prepare to create a Middle Eastern masterpiece that will delight all your senses.
